title: Pyramus et Thisbe
source: Ovid, Metamorphoses 4.55–166. Latin text: the pre-1930 vulgate (after Hugo Magnus, ed., Berlin: Weidmann, 1914), public domain; verified line by line. Original spelling kept (conp-, adsp-, isdem, oraque). Pronunciation convention: reconstructed Classical (restored) Latin, applied consistently. All translations were made fresh from the Latin for Lectorium.
about: Pyramus and Thisbe are the young Babylonian lovers whose story Ovid sets into the Metamorphoses (told by one of the daughters of Minyas). Forbidden to marry, they whisper through a crack in the wall between their houses and agree to meet by night at the tomb of Ninus, beneath a white-fruited mulberry — and the tale turns on how that fruit came to be dark. It is the ancestor of Romeo and Juliet and of the 'Pyramus and Thisbe' play in A Midsummer Night's Dream. The Latin is dactylic hexameter, and Ovid's sentences run freely across the verse lines (enjambment), so the unit you tap often spans several lines; the line numbers are the standard way to cite the poem. There is no text-to-speech for Latin here — reading is by eye.

sentences.t:
Pyramus et Thisbe, iuvenum pulcherrimus alter,
altera, quas Oriens habuit, praelata puellis,
contiguas tenuere domos, ubi dicitur altam
coctilibus muris cinxisse Semiramis urbem.
sentences.l:
Pyramus and Thisbe — the one the fairest of young men,
the other preferred above the girls the East has held —
held neighboring houses, where, it is said, the lofty
city Semiramis ringed about with walls of brick.
sentences.i: Pyramus and Thisbe — he the handsomest of young men, she the loveliest of all the girls the East could show — lived in adjoining houses, in the city Semiramis is said to have ringed about with high walls of brick.
sentences.n: The two names come first, then split apart across the line: 'the one (alter)… the other (altera)' — a balanced pairing Ovid stretches over four lines before the main verb 'tenuere' (they held) finally lands. 'quas Oriens habuit' = 'whom the East held,' i.e. of all Eastern women. Semiramis was the legendary queen who built Babylon; 'coctilibus muris' (with baked/brick walls) points to the city's famous brickwork. The tale is set in Babylon.

sentences.t:
notitiam primosque gradus vicinia fecit,
tempore crevit amor;
sentences.l:
Nearness made their acquaintance and first steps;
in time love grew;
sentences.i: Living side by side first brought them together and led to the first steps of love; and with time that love grew;
sentences.n: 'vicinia fecit' — 'neighborhood made (it)': proximity is the subject, the cause. 'primos gradus' (first steps) is a walking metaphor for a budding relationship. Note how Ovid marks the stages with bare, quick verbs — 'fecit… crevit' (made… grew).

sentences.t:
taedae quoque iure coissent,
sed vetuere patres: quod non potuere vetare,
ex aequo captis ardebant mentibus ambo.
sentences.l:
by the marriage-torch's right they would have joined as well,
but their fathers forbade it; what they could not forbid —
both alike were ablaze, their hearts taken captive.
sentences.i: and they would have married, too, but their fathers forbade it. Yet what the fathers could not forbid was this: both blazed alike, their hearts taken captive.
sentences.n: 'taedae iure' — 'by the right of the wedding-torch': the torch (taeda) stands in for marriage itself, a metonymy you meet constantly in Latin poetry. 'coissent' (pluperfect subjunctive) = 'would have joined (in marriage).' 'vetuere' and 'potuere' are the older poetic form of 'vetuerunt / potuerunt' — the 3rd-person plural perfect in -ēre, extremely common in verse (you already saw 'tenuere' above; watch for it throughout). The turn: fathers can forbid a marriage, not love.

sentences.t:
conscius omnis abest; nutu signisque loquuntur,
quoque magis tegitur, tectus magis aestuat ignis.
sentences.l:
Every confidant is absent; by nod and signs they speak,
and the more it is covered, the more, covered, the fire seethes.
sentences.i: They have no go-between; they speak by nods and signs — and the more the fire is smothered, the more, smothered, it blazes.
sentences.n: 'conscius' = one who is 'in the know,' an accomplice or confidant. 'quoque magis… magis' = 'the more… the more.' The buried fire that burns hotter for being covered ('tegitur… tectus,' cover/covered) is a stock image of hidden love, sharpened here by the near-repeated words.

sentences.t:
fissus erat tenui rima, quam duxerat olim,
cum fieret, paries domui communis utrique.
sentences.l:
Split by a thin crack, which it had drawn long ago
when it was being built, was the wall shared by both houses.
sentences.i: The wall shared by the two houses had a thin crack in it, which it had developed long before, back when it was first built.
sentences.n: The subject 'paries' (wall) is held back to the very end, after everything that describes it — normal in Latin verse, where the key noun often arrives late. 'fissus erat' (had been split) is a passive pluperfect. 'quam' looks back to 'rima' (the crack); 'duxerat' ('had drawn/led') is the ordinary verb for a crack forming. 'cum fieret' = 'when it was being made' (cum + imperfect subjunctive).

sentences.t:
id vitium nulli per saecula longa notatum -
quid non sentit amor? - primi vidistis amantes
et vocis fecistis iter, tutaeque per illud
murmure blanditiae minimo transire solebant.
sentences.l:
That flaw, marked by no one through the long ages —
what does love not notice? — you lovers first saw it
and made a passage for your voice; and safely through it,
in the faintest murmur, endearments used to cross.
sentences.i: That flaw, which no one had noticed in all the long years — but what does love fail to notice? — you lovers were the first to see, and you made it a passage for your voices; and through it, safe and in the faintest whisper, your sweet words would cross to and fro.
sentences.n: Ovid suddenly turns from the story to address the lovers directly — 'vidistis… fecistis' ('you saw… you made,' 2nd person plural). This apostrophe pulls the reader into sympathy with them. The dashes set off 'quid non sentit amor?' ('what does love not perceive?') as a knowing aside. 'vitium' = a defect or flaw (here the crack); 'blanditiae' = coaxing words, sweet-talk, endearments.

sentences.t:
saepe, ubi constiterant hinc Thisbe, Pyramus illinc,
inque vices fuerat captatus anhelitus oris,
"invide" dicebant "paries, quid amantibus obstas?
sentences.l:
Often, when they had stood — here Thisbe, Pyramus there —
and by turns the breath of the mouth had been caught,
they would say: 'Envious wall, why block lovers?
sentences.i: Often, when they had taken their places — Thisbe on this side, Pyramus on that — and each in turn had caught the other's breath, they would say: 'Spiteful wall, why do you block two lovers?
sentences.n: 'hinc… illinc' = 'on this side… on that side.' 'inque vices' = 'and in turn, by turns.' 'anhelitus oris' = 'the breath of the mouth,' caught through the crack — they breathe each other's air. Then they scold the wall itself ('invide… paries,' vocative): addressing a lifeless thing as though it could hear is another apostrophe, matching Ovid's own just above. 'obstas' (you block) takes the dative 'amantibus' (lovers).

sentences.t:
quantum erat, ut sineres toto nos corpore iungi
aut, hoc si nimium est, vel ad oscula danda pateres?
sentences.l:
How much would it be, to let us join with our whole body —
or, if this is too much, at least stand open for kisses?
sentences.i: What would it have cost you to let us join, body to body — or, if that is too much to ask, at least to open wide enough for us to kiss?
sentences.n: 'quantum erat, ut…?' is an idiom of reproach: literally 'how great a thing was it, that…?', i.e. 'would it really have been so much to ask that…?' 'toto corpore iungi' — 'to be joined with the whole body,' to embrace fully. 'ad oscula danda' — 'for kisses to-be-given': ad + gerundive expresses purpose ('for the giving of kisses').

sentences.t:
nec sumus ingrati: tibi nos debere fatemur,
quod datus est verbis ad amicas transitus auris."
sentences.l:
Nor are we ungrateful: we confess we owe you this,
that a crossing was granted for our words to loving ears.'
sentences.i: Yet we are not ungrateful: we admit we are in your debt for letting our words cross over to a loved one's ears.'
sentences.n: 'nec sumus ingrati' softens the scolding — the lovers thank the wall even as they blame it. 'tibi nos debere fatemur' = 'we admit that we owe (it) to you' (accusative-and-infinitive after 'fatemur,' we confess). 'amicas auris' = 'the loving ears' ('auris' is the poetic accusative plural, = 'aures').

sentences.t:
talia diversa nequiquam sede locuti
sub noctem dixere "vale" partique dedere
oscula quisque suae non pervenientia contra.
sentences.l:
Having spoken such things in vain from their separate sides,
toward night they said 'farewell,' and each to their own side
gave kisses that did not reach across to the other.
sentences.i: So they would talk, in vain, from their separate sides; and as night came on they said their goodbyes, each pressing on their own face of the wall kisses that never reached the other.
sentences.n: 'diversa sede' = 'from a separate position' (ablative of place). 'nequiquam' = 'in vain, to no purpose' — the whole exchange is futile. 'sub noctem' = 'toward night, as night came on.' The pathos of the close: 'oscula… non pervenientia contra' — kisses 'not reaching across,' each stopped by the wall. 'dixere… dedere' are again the -ēre perfects (= dixerunt, dederunt).

sentences.t:
postera nocturnos Aurora removerat ignes,
solque pruinosas radiis siccaverat herbas:
ad solitum coiere locum.
sentences.l:
The next Dawn had removed the fires of night,
and the sun with its rays had dried the frosty grass:
to their accustomed place they came together.
sentences.i: Dawn came and put out the fires of night, and the sun's rays dried the frost from the grass; and they met at their usual place.
sentences.n: A grand two-line way of saying 'day broke.' 'Aurora' (Dawn) and 'sol' (the sun) act as agents; 'nocturnos ignes' ('the fires of night') = the stars. This elevated way of marking sunrise and sunset is an epic convention Ovid uses (and gently overuses). 'coiere' = coierunt (they came together) — another -ēre perfect.

sentences.t:
tum murmure parvo
multa prius questi statuunt, ut nocte silenti
fallere custodes foribusque excedere temptent,
cumque domo exierint, urbis quoque tecta relinquant,
neve sit errandum lato spatiantibus arvo,
conveniant ad busta Nini lateantque sub umbra
arboris:
sentences.l:
then, in a low murmur,
having first lamented much, they resolve that in the silent night
they will try to trick their guards and slip out of doors,
and once they have left the house, leave the city's roofs too;
and, lest they must wander roaming over the wide plain,
they meet at the tomb of Ninus and hide beneath the shade
of a tree:
sentences.i: then, whispering, and after many complaints, they make their plan: that night, in the silence, they would try to slip past their guards and out the doors; and once out of the house, to leave the town behind as well; and, so as not to lose each other roaming the open country, they would meet at the tomb of Ninus and hide in the shade of a tree —
sentences.n: One long sentence of purpose-clauses after 'statuunt' (they decide): 'ut… temptent… relinquant… conveniant… lateant' ('that they should try… leave… meet… hide'), all subjunctive. 'neve sit errandum' = 'and lest there be wandering to-be-done,' an impersonal gerundive ('so they should not have to wander'). 'busta Nini' = the tomb-mound of Ninus, Semiramis's husband and Babylon's founder — a fittingly grand and funereal meeting place.

sentences.t:
arbor ibi niveis uberrima pomis,
ardua morus, erat, gelido contermina fonti.
sentences.l:
a tree there, richest in snow-white fruit —
a tall mulberry — stood, bordering a cool spring.
sentences.i: for by a cool spring there stood a tall mulberry, laden with snow-white berries.
sentences.n: Remember this tree: its fruit is now white ('niveis… pomis,' snow-white), and the whole tale exists to explain why mulberries are dark. 'uberrima' (most fruitful, laden) is the superlative of 'uber.' 'morus' (mulberry) is feminine, so 'ardua' (tall) agrees with it. The subject 'arbor… morus… erat' is again spread out, with the verb 'erat' held in the middle.

sentences.t:
pacta placent; et lux, tarde discedere visa,
praecipitatur aquis, et aquis nox exit ab isdem.
sentences.l:
The plan pleases them; and the light, slow (it seemed) to leave,
is plunged into the waters, and from the same waters night comes out.
sentences.i: The plan pleased them both; and the daylight, so slow (it seemed) to go, plunged at last beneath the waves, and from those same waters night rose up.
sentences.n: 'pacta' = 'the things agreed on,' the plan (from paciscor, to make a bargain). Another sunset-into-the-Ocean image, neatly balanced: day 'is plunged into the waters,' night 'comes out of the same waters' — the sun and stars were imagined sinking into and rising from the Ocean at the world's rim. 'isdem' = 'iisdem' (from the same).

sentences.t:
Callida per tenebras versato cardine Thisbe
egreditur fallitque suos adopertaque vultum
pervenit ad tumulum dictaque sub arbore sedit.
sentences.l:
Clever Thisbe, through the dark, the hinge turned,
goes out and deceives her household and, her face veiled,
reaches the tomb and sat beneath the appointed tree.
sentences.i: Bold and clever, Thisbe opened the door in the dark, slipped out unseen past her household, and with her face veiled came to the tomb and sat down beneath the appointed tree.
sentences.n: 'versato cardine' — 'the hinge having been turned,' an ablative absolute (a self-contained 'with X done' phrase); Latin loves these where English uses a clause. 'adoperta vultum' = 'covered as to her face' — the accusative 'vultum' after a passive participle is the 'Greek accusative' (accusative of respect), very common in poetry for parts of the body. Note the tense-shift from present ('egreditur') to perfect ('sedit') — Ovid moves fluidly between vivid present and past.

sentences.i: Love made her brave. But look — up comes a lioness, her foaming jaws freshly smeared with the blood of slaughtered oxen, meaning to slake her thirst at the nearby spring;
sentences.n: 'audacem faciebat amor' — 'love was making (her) bold': the imperfect holds the scene open just before it shatters. 'ecce' ('look! behold!') jolts us into the danger. 'oblita… rictus' = 'besmeared as to her jaws' — again a Greek accusative ('rictus,' the open jaws, 4th-declension accusative plural). 'depositura' is a future participle ('about to / meaning to put down her thirst'), a compact Latin way of saying 'in order to drink.'

sentences.t:
quam procul ad lunae radios Babylonia Thisbe
vidit et obscurum timido pede fugit in antrum,
dumque fugit, tergo velamina lapsa reliquit.
sentences.l:
Her, far off, by the moon's rays, Babylonian Thisbe
saw, and on fearful foot fled into a dark cave,
and as she flees, left her veil, slipped from her back.
sentences.i: Babylonian Thisbe saw her from far off by the moonlight and fled on trembling feet into a dark cave; and as she ran, her veil slipped from her shoulders and she left it behind.
sentences.n: 'quam' (whom) heads the sentence, reaching back to the lioness — Latin often opens with a relative pronoun linking to what came before ('and her…'). 'timido pede' ('with fearful foot') is a singular for plural and a transferred epithet: it is Thisbe who is afraid, not her foot. 'tergo… lapsa' = 'slipped from her back'; the veil ('velamina,' poetic plural) is what the lioness will find.

sentences.t:
ut lea saeva sitim multa conpescuit unda,
dum redit in silvas, inventos forte sine ipsa
ore cruentato tenues laniavit amictus.
sentences.l:
When the fierce lioness had quenched her thirst with much water,
as she returns to the woods — found by chance without its owner —
with bloodied mouth she tore the flimsy garments.
sentences.i: When the fierce lioness had quenched her thirst with deep draughts, on her way back into the woods she came by chance on the flimsy veil — lying there without its owner — and ripped it apart in her blood-stained jaws.
sentences.n: 'conpescuit' = compescuit ('checked, quenched') — the spelling 'conp-' (for 'comp-') is the older form this edition keeps. 'inventos… sine ipsa' — the veil, 'found without (Thisbe) herself,' i.e. lying there empty of its owner: 'ipsa' = 'the woman herself,' the fatal gap. 'ore cruentato' = 'with bloodied mouth' (ablative). This bloodied cloth, not Thisbe, is what Pyramus is about to find.

sentences.i: Pyramus, who had come out later, saw the beast's unmistakable tracks in the deep dust, and the color drained from his whole face;
sentences.n: The subject 'Pyramus' is held back to the start of the next line, landing after we already watch him turn pale — a small stab of suspense. 'certa… vestigia ferae' = 'the beast's sure (unmistakable) tracks.' 'toto… ore' = 'over his whole face' (ablative). 'expalluit' = 'went pale' (the 'ex-' intensifies): the body's sudden sign of dread.

sentences.t:
ut vero vestem quoque sanguine tinctam
repperit, "una duos" inquit "nox perdet amantes,
sentences.l:
but when the garment too, stained with blood,
he found, 'One night,' he says, 'will destroy two lovers,
sentences.i: But when he found the veil as well, stained with blood, he cried: 'A single night will be the death of two lovers,
sentences.n: 'ut… repperit' = 'when he found' (ut + perfect indicative = 'when'). 'vero' ('but, indeed') marks the turn from dread to grim certainty. 'inquit' ('he says') is dropped inside the quotation, as Latin regularly places verbs of speaking. 'una… nox… duos amantes' — 'one night, two lovers': the bare numbers frame his despair; he leaps at once to the wrong conclusion.

sentences.t:
e quibus illa fuit longa dignissima vita;
nostra nocens anima est.
sentences.l:
of whom she was most worthy of a long life;
mine is the guilty soul.
sentences.i: and of the two, she was the one most deserving of a long life; mine is the guilty soul.
sentences.n: 'e quibus' = 'of whom' (of the two lovers). 'dignissima' takes the ablative 'longa vita' = 'most worthy of a long life.' 'nostra… anima' — 'our soul,' a poetic plural for 'my life/soul'; Pyramus condemns himself before he even knows the truth.

sentences.t:
ego te, miseranda, peremi,
in loca plena metus qui iussi nocte venires
nec prior huc veni.
sentences.l:
I, poor girl, have destroyed you —
I who ordered you to come by night into places full of fear,
and did not come here first.
sentences.i: It was I who killed you, poor love — I, who told you to come by night to a place so full of danger, and then failed to get here first.
sentences.n: 'miseranda' = '(you) to be pitied,' a gerundive used as a tender address ('poor girl'). 'qui iussi… venires' — 'I who ordered (that) you should come': after 'iussi' the subjunctive 'venires' works like 'ordered you to come.' The three charges pile up: I killed you, I summoned you into danger, I came too late.

sentences.t:
nostrum divellite corpus
et scelerata fero consumite viscera morsu,
o quicumque sub hac habitatis rupe leones!
sentences.l:
Tear apart my body,
and devour my guilty flesh with savage bite,
O whatever lions dwell beneath this crag!
sentences.i: Tear my body apart, you lions — whatever lions den beneath this cliff — and devour my guilty flesh in your savage jaws!
sentences.n: He calls on the lions to punish him — 'divellite… consumite' are plural imperatives ('tear!… devour!'). 'o quicumque… leones' = 'O whatever lions,' addressing them directly (vocative, and yet another apostrophe). 'scelerata… viscera' = 'guilty flesh' ('viscera' = the soft inner flesh); 'fero… morsu' = 'with savage bite,' the words split apart around the phrase they enclose — a hallmark of Latin verse word-order.

sentences.t:
sed timidi est optare necem." velamina Thisbes
tollit et ad pactae secum fert arboris umbram,
sentences.l:
But to wish for death is a coward's part.' Thisbe's veil
he lifts and carries with him to the appointed tree's shade,
sentences.i: 'But it is a coward's part only to wish for death.' He picked up Thisbe's veil and carried it with him into the shade of the appointed tree;
sentences.n: 'timidi est' = 'it is (the mark) of a coward,' a genitive of characteristic ('it belongs to a coward to…'). Pyramus shames himself out of mere words and into the act. 'Thisbes' is a Greek genitive ('of Thisbe'); Greek names in Latin keep their Greek endings. 'ad… arboris umbram' — 'to the shade of the tree,' with 'arboris' and 'umbram' wrapped around 'pactae' (agreed-upon).

sentences.t:
utque dedit notae lacrimas, dedit oscula vesti,
"accipe nunc" inquit "nostri quoque sanguinis haustus!"
sentences.l:
and when he had given tears to the known cloth, and kisses to the garment,
'now drink,' he says, 'draughts of my blood as well!'
sentences.i: and when he had wept over the cloth he knew so well and kissed it, he said: 'Now drink my blood as well!'
sentences.n: The doubled 'dedit… dedit' ('he gave… he gave' — tears, then kisses) is anaphora, the repetition heightening the ritual of grief. 'notae… vesti' = 'to the familiar garment' (dative). 'nostri… sanguinis haustus' = 'draughts of my blood' ('haustus,' drinks, accusative plural): he tells the veil, already soaked with what he takes for Thisbe's blood, to drink his too.

sentences.t:
quoque erat accinctus, demisit in ilia ferrum,
nec mora, ferventi moriens e vulnere traxit.
sentences.l:
and the blade he was girded with he sank into his groin,
and at once, dying, drew it from the seething wound.
sentences.i: and the sword at his side he drove into his own belly, and at once, dying, pulled it back out of the seething wound.
sentences.n: Careful: 'quoque' here is NOT the common word 'quoque' (also). It is 'quō-que' = 'and with which' (the ablative of the relative 'quī' + '-que'): 'the sword with which he was girded.' The same letters, a different word — a trap worth remembering. 'ferventi… e vulnere' = 'from the boiling wound,' the blood hot as it gushes; 'moriens' ('dying,' present participle) makes the act and the death one moment.

sentences.t:
ut iacuit resupinus humo, cruor emicat alte,
non aliter quam cum vitiato fistula plumbo
scinditur et tenui stridente foramine longas
eiaculatur aquas atque ictibus aera rumpit.
sentences.l:
As he lay back on the ground, the blood spurts up high,
not otherwise than when, its lead being faulty, a pipe
splits and through a thin hissing hole shoots out long
jets of water and bursts the air with its bursts.
sentences.i: As he lay sprawled on his back, his blood shot high into the air — just as when a lead water-pipe splits at a flaw and through the narrow hissing crack sends a long jet spurting out, cutting the air in bursts.
sentences.n: A famously startling simile: the arc of spurting blood is likened to water bursting from a cracked lead pipe — an everyday, almost mechanical image dropped into high tragedy, very Ovidian. 'non aliter quam cum' = 'not otherwise than when,' i.e. 'just as when,' the standard formula that opens an epic simile. 'vitiato… plumbo' = 'the lead being faulty' (ablative absolute). Listen to the hissing packed into 'tenui stridente… scinditur' — the verse imitates the sound.

sentences.t:
arborei fetus adspergine caedis in atram
vertuntur faciem, madefactaque sanguine radix
purpureo tinguit pendentia mora colore.
sentences.l:
The tree's fruit, by the spray of slaughter, into a dark
hue is turned, and the root, soaked with blood,
dyes the hanging mulberries a purple color.
sentences.i: The tree's fruit, splashed by the spurting blood, turns dark; and the root, soaked with blood, stains the hanging berries a deep purple-red.
sentences.n: The metamorphosis the whole tale exists to explain: white mulberries turn dark. 'arborei fetus' = 'the tree's offspring,' a lofty way to say 'its fruit.' 'adspergine caedis' = 'by the sprinkling of slaughter' (the blood-spray); 'adsp-' is the older spelling of 'asp-.' 'purpureo… colore' = 'with a purple color' (Latin 'purpureus' ranges from crimson to dark red). The change rises through the roots as well as falling in the spray.

sentences.i: But look — her terror not yet gone, yet unwilling to fail her lover, Thisbe comes back, searching for the young man with her eyes and her heart, longing to tell what great dangers she has just escaped;
sentences.n: 'metu nondum posito' = 'her fear not yet set aside' (ablative absolute). 'ne fallat amantem' = 'lest she fail/disappoint her lover' — she comes back despite her fear so as not to let Pyramus down. 'quanta… vitarit… pericula' = 'how great dangers she had escaped' is an indirect question ('vitarit' = vitaverit, perfect subjunctive) after 'narrare gestit' (she longs to tell).

sentences.t:
utque locum et visa cognoscit in arbore formam,
sic facit incertam pomi color: haeret, an haec sit.
sentences.l:
and as she knows the place and the shape seen in the tree,
so the fruit's color makes her unsure: she hesitates — is this it?
sentences.i: and though she recognizes the place and the shape of the tree, the color of the fruit leaves her unsure; she hesitates — can this be the right one?
sentences.n: 'utque… sic' = 'and as… so': she knows the spot, but ('sic facit incertam') the changed fruit-color makes her doubt. 'haeret, an haec sit' = 'she sticks fast (hesitates) whether this is (the tree)' — 'an… sit' is an indirect question in the subjunctive. The dark berries she cannot yet explain are already unsettling her.

sentences.t:
dum dubitat, tremebunda videt pulsare cruentum
membra solum, retroque pedem tulit, oraque buxo
pallidiora gerens exhorruit aequoris instar,
quod tremit, exigua cum summum stringitur aura.
sentences.l:
While she hesitates, trembling, she sees the bloodied
ground thrashed by limbs, and drew her foot back; and her face, than boxwood
paler, she shuddered like the surface of the sea,
which trembles when its top is skimmed by a slight breeze.
sentences.i: As she wavers, trembling, she sees limbs writhing on the bloodstained ground, and shrank back; and with a face paler than boxwood she shuddered like the surface of the sea when a faint breeze skims across it and it quivers.
sentences.n: 'tremebunda' ('trembling') and 'exhorruit' ('shuddered') frame her whole-body horror. 'membra' ('limbs') — not yet 'Pyramus': she sees only thrashing limbs on the dark ground before she understands. 'oraque buxo pallidiora' = 'a face paler than boxwood' (buxus, a pale-yellow wood; ablative of comparison). Then a delicate simile: she shivers 'like the sea' ('aequoris instar') that ripples at the lightest breeze — sudden calm turned to a tremor.

sentences.t:
sed postquam remorata suos cognovit amores,
percutit indignos claro plangore lacertos
et laniata comas amplexaque corpus amatum
vulnera supplevit lacrimis fletumque cruori
miscuit
sentences.l:
But when, after a moment, she knew her own beloved,
she beats her undeserving arms with loud beating,
and, her hair torn and the beloved body embraced,
she filled his wounds with tears, and weeping with blood
she mixed,
sentences.i: But when, after a moment, she recognized her own beloved, she beat her innocent arms with loud blows and tore her hair; and clasping the body she loved, she filled his wounds with tears, mingling her weeping with his blood,
sentences.n: The formal gestures of ancient mourning: beating the arms, tearing the hair. 'indignos… lacertos' = 'undeserving arms' — they did nothing to deserve the blows, a poignant touch. 'laniata comas' = 'having torn her hair,' with 'comas' a Greek accusative of respect again ('torn as to her hair'). 'remorata,' 'laniata,' 'amplexa' are deponent participles — passive in form, active in meaning ('having lingered / torn / embraced').

sentences.t:
et gelidis in vultibus oscula figens
"Pyrame," clamavit, "quis te mihi casus ademit?
Pyrame, responde! tua te carissima Thisbe
nominat; exaudi vultusque attolle iacentes!"
sentences.l:
and, planting kisses on his cold face,
'Pyramus,' she cried, 'what chance has taken you from me?
Pyramus, answer! Your own dearest Thisbe
names you; hear me, and lift up your fallen face!'
sentences.i: and pressing kisses on his cold face, she cried: 'Pyramus! What accident has torn you from me? Pyramus, answer me! It is your own dearest Thisbe calling your name — listen, and lift your fallen face!'
sentences.n: 'gelidis in vultibus' = 'on his cold face' ('vultus,' plural for singular). 'quis… casus' = 'what mishap, what accident.' 'te mihi ademit' = 'took you from me' (dative 'mihi' of the person deprived). She names herself — 'tua… carissima Thisbe' — and calls him back with imperatives 'responde… exaudi… attolle' ('answer… hear… lift up'), as if love could reach the dead.

sentences.t:
ad nomen Thisbes oculos a morte gravatos
Pyramus erexit visaque recondidit illa.
sentences.l:
At the name of Thisbe, his eyes, heavy with death,
Pyramus raised, and, having seen her, closed them again.
sentences.i: At the name of Thisbe, Pyramus lifted his eyes, already heavy with death; he looked on her once — and closed them again.
sentences.n: A single unbearable moment: he opens his eyes at her name ('ad nomen Thisbes,' Greek genitive again), sees her, and dies. 'oculos… gravatos' = 'his eyes, weighed down (by death).' 'visa… illa' = 'she having been seen' (ablative absolute) — he 'buried' his gaze again ('recondidit,' hid away) the instant he had seen her. Recognition and death arrive together.

sentences.t:
Quae postquam vestemque suam cognovit et ense
vidit ebur vacuum, "tua te manus" inquit "amorque
perdidit, infelix!
sentences.l:
And she, when she recognized her own veil and saw
the ivory empty of its sword, 'your own hand,' she says, 'and love
have destroyed you, poor man!
sentences.i: But when she recognized her own veil and saw the ivory scabbard empty of its blade, she said: 'Your own hand and your own love have killed you, my poor love!
sentences.n: 'Quae' opens the sentence as a connecting relative = 'And she' (Latin often begins a new sentence with a relative pronoun where English would start fresh). 'ense… ebur vacuum' = 'the ivory (scabbard) empty of its sword' (ablative of separation) — she reads the whole story from the empty sheath. 'tua te manus… amorque perdidit' — 'your hand and your love destroyed you': the verb 'perdidit' is singular though it has two subjects, agreeing with the nearer one, and the hissing 'tua te… amorque' drives the reproach home.

sentences.t:
est et mihi fortis in unum
hoc manus, est et amor: dabit hic in vulnera vires.
persequar extinctum letique miserrima dicar
causa comesque tui:
sentences.l:
I too have a hand strong for this one thing,
and I too have love: it will give strength for the wounds.
I will follow you, dead, and, most wretched, be called
the cause and companion of your death;
sentences.i: I too have a hand brave enough for this one deed, and I have love as well: it will give me the strength to strike. I will follow you into death, and be called — most wretched — the cause and the companion of your dying;
sentences.n: She turns his own logic on herself: 'est et mihi… manus, est et amor' — 'I too have a hand… I too have love,' the repeated 'est et' balancing her resolve against his. 'in unum hoc' = 'for this one act' (her suicide). 'extinctum' ('put out, extinguished') = the dead Pyramus. 'letique… causa comesque tui' = 'the cause and companion of your death' ('leti,' genitive of 'letum,' death). 'persequar… dicar' are futures ('I shall follow… I shall be called').

sentences.t:
quique a me morte revelli
heu sola poteras, poteris nec morte revelli.
sentences.l:
and you who could be torn from me by death alone —
alas — you will not be torn away even by death.
sentences.i: and you, whom death alone had the power to tear from me — not even death, now, shall part us.
sentences.n: A paradox built on repetition: 'morte revelli… poteras… poteris nec morte revelli' — 'you could be torn away by death… you will not be able to be torn away by death.' Death was the one thing that could separate them; by dying too, she cancels even that. The near-repeated line frames the vow, 'nec… revelli' overturning 'poteras… revelli.'

sentences.t:
hoc tamen amborum verbis estote rogati,
o multum miseri meus illiusque parentes,
ut, quos certus amor, quos hora novissima iunxit,
conponi tumulo non invideatis eodem;
sentences.l:
Yet be entreated this one thing, by the words of us both,
O deeply wretched parents, mine and his:
that those whom sure love, whom the last hour has joined,
you not begrudge to be laid together in one tomb;
sentences.i: Yet grant us both this one prayer, O parents, mine and his, so deep in sorrow: those whom true love and the last hour have joined — do not begrudge us a place together in a single tomb;
sentences.n: 'estote rogati' = 'be (you) entreated,' a future imperative plural, solemn and formal, addressed to both sets of parents ('o… parentes,' vocative). 'conponi… non invideatis' = 'do not begrudge (us) to be laid together' ('conp-' for 'comp-' again). The twin relative clauses 'quos… amor, quos… hora… iunxit' ('whom love, whom the last hour joined') balance love against death as the two forces that unite the pair.

sentences.t:
at tu quae ramis arbor miserabile corpus
nunc tegis unius, mox es tectura duorum,
signa tene caedis pullosque et luctibus aptos
semper habe fetus, gemini monimenta cruoris."
sentences.l:
but you, tree, who with your branches the piteous body
now shelter of one, and soon will shelter of two,
keep the marks of our death, and dark, fit for grief,
have your fruit forever, memorials of a twin bloodshed.'
sentences.i: And you, tree, whose branches now shade the piteous body of one, and soon will shade two — keep the marks of our death: bear always dark fruit, fit for mourning, in memory of our double blood.'
sentences.n: She addresses the mulberry directly ('at tu… arbor,' vocative and apostrophe once more) and lays on it a kind of blessing-curse: 'signa tene… semper habe fetus' ('keep the tokens… bear fruit always,' imperatives) — the tree is to wear dark berries forever as a memorial. 'es tectura' = 'you are going to cover' (a future active periphrastic). 'pullos… fetus' = 'dark fruits'; 'gemini… cruoris' = 'of a twin bloodshed,' the two deaths made one.

sentences.t:
dixit et aptato pectus mucrone sub imum
incubuit ferro, quod adhuc a caede tepebat.
sentences.l:
She spoke, and, the point set beneath the bottom of her breast,
leaned upon the blade, which was still warm from the slaughter.
sentences.i: She spoke, and setting the sword-point beneath her breast, she fell forward onto the blade — still warm with his blood.
sentences.n: 'aptato… mucrone' = 'the point having been fitted/set (in place),' ablative absolute. 'pectus sub imum' = 'beneath the bottom of her breast' ('imum pectus,' the lower breast). 'incubuit ferro' = 'she leaned/fell upon the iron' (incumbo + dative). The detail that the blade is 'still warm from his death' ('a caede tepebat') fuses their two suicides into one act on one weapon.

sentences.i: Yet their prayers did move the gods, and moved their parents too: for the mulberry, when it is fully ripe, is dark; and what the pyres left of them rests together in a single urn.
sentences.n: The doubled 'tetigere… tetigere' ('they touched… they touched,' = tetigerunt) grants both prayers at once — the gods darken the fruit, the parents share the urn. 'ubi permaturuit' = 'when it has ripened fully' (the 'per-' intensifies). 'quod… rogis superest' = 'what survives the funeral pyres,' i.e. the ashes; 'una… in urna' = 'in one urn,' the last words giving the lovers in death the union that life denied them.

glossary.pyramus: Pyramus — the young man of the tale (nom.)
glossary.et: et — and; et… et: both… and
glossary.thisbe: Thisbē — the heroine (nom./voc.; a Greek name)
glossary.pulcherrimus: pulcher — most beautiful, handsomest (superl., m. nom.)
glossary.quas: quī — whom, which (rel., f. acc. pl.)
glossary.oriens: oriens — the East, the rising sun (nom.)
glossary.praelata: praeferō — preferred, set above (perf. part., f. nom.) + dat.
glossary.puellis: puella — girls (dat./abl. pl.); here 'above the girls'
glossary.tenuere: teneō — they held, occupied (= tenuerunt, poetic -ēre perfect)
glossary.cinxisse: cingō — to have girded, ringed around (perf. inf.)
glossary.semiramis: Semīramis — legendary queen who built Babylon (nom.)
glossary.taedae: taeda — of the wedding-torch (gen.); taeda = marriage
glossary.quoque: quoque — also, too; BUT in l.119 = quō-que 'and with which' (abl. of quī + -que)
glossary.iure: iūs — by right, lawfully (abl.); taedae iure: in lawful marriage
glossary.coissent: coeō — they would have joined (in marriage) (pluperf. subj.)
glossary.ex: ex — out of, from; ex aequo: equally
glossary.aequo: aequus — level, equal (n. abl.); ex aequo: on equal terms
glossary.captis: capiō — captured, taken (perf. part., f. abl. pl.)
glossary.conscius: conscius — a confidant, one 'in the know' (nom.)
glossary.magis: magis — more; quo magis… magis: the more… the more
glossary.fissus: findō — split, cleft (perf. part., m. nom.)
glossary.rima: rīma — a crack, chink (here abl., 'by a crack')
glossary.quam: quī — which, whom (rel., f. acc.); also 'than'; 'how'
glossary.duxerat: dūcō — had drawn, developed (pluperf.); of a crack forming
glossary.fieret: fīō — it was being made (impf. subj.)
glossary.longa: longus — long (n. acc. pl. / f. abl.)
glossary.notatum: notō — noticed, marked (perf. part., n. nom.)
glossary.illud: ille — that (n. acc.); per illud: through it
glossary.solebant: soleō — they used to, were accustomed (impf.)
glossary.constiterant: cōnsistō — they had taken their stand (pluperf.)
glossary.hinc: hinc — from here, on this side
glossary.illinc: illinc — from there, on that side
glossary.inque: in — and in (+ -que); inque vices: and in turn
glossary.vices: vicis — turns (acc. pl.); inque vices: by turns
glossary.captatus: captō — caught, snatched at (perf. part., m. nom.)
glossary.obstas: obstō — you stand in the way, block (+ dat.)
glossary.quantum: quantus — how much (n.); quantum erat: what would it have cost?
glossary.ut: ut — that, so that (+ subj.); when; as
glossary.toto: tōtus — whole (n. abl.); toto corpore: with the whole body
glossary.ad: ad — to, toward; for (+ gerundive of purpose)
glossary.danda: dō — to be given (gerundive, n. acc. pl.); ad oscula danda: for giving kisses
glossary.pateres: pateō — you would stand open (impf. subj.)
glossary.nec: nec — nor, and not; nec… nec: neither… nor
glossary.datus: dō — given, granted (perf. part., m. nom.)
glossary.auris: auris — to the ears (acc. pl. = aures)
glossary.diversa: dīversus — separate, apart (f. abl.); diversa sede: from separate sides
glossary.nequiquam: nēquīquam — in vain, to no purpose
glossary.locuti: loquor — having spoken (deponent perf. part., m. nom. pl.)
glossary.sub: sub — under; (of time) toward; sub noctem: as night comes on
glossary.pervenientia: perveniō — reaching, arriving (pres. part., n. acc. pl.)
glossary.contra: contrā — across, to the opposite side
glossary.postera: posterus — the next, following (f. nom.); postera Aurora: next dawn
glossary.nocturnos: nocturnus — of the night, nocturnal (m. acc. pl.)
glossary.ignes: ignis — fires (acc. pl.); nocturnos ignes: the stars
glossary.coiere: coeō — they came together, met (= coierunt)
glossary.parvo: parvus — small, low (n. abl.); murmure parvo: in a low murmur
glossary.multa: multus — many things, much (n. acc. pl.)
glossary.questi: queror — having complained, lamented (deponent perf. part., m. nom. pl.)
glossary.silenti: silēns — silent (abl.); nocte silenti: in the silent night
glossary.temptent: temptō — they should try, attempt (pres. subj.)
glossary.exierint: exeō — they will have gone out (fut. perf. / perf. subj.)
glossary.relinquant: relinquō — they should leave behind (pres. subj.)
glossary.neve: nēve — and lest, and so that not
glossary.errandum: errō — wandering (gerundive, n.); sit errandum: there be need to wander
glossary.lato: lātus — wide, broad (n. abl.); lato arvo: over the wide plain
glossary.spatiantibus: spatior — to (them) roaming (deponent pres. part., dat. pl.)
glossary.conveniant: conveniō — they should meet, come together (pres. subj.)
glossary.nini: Nīnus — of Ninus (gen.), founder of Babylon, husband of Semiramis
glossary.lateantque: lateō — and they should lie hidden (pres. subj. + -que)
glossary.niveis: niveus — snow-white (n. abl. pl.); niveis pomis: with snow-white fruit
glossary.uberrima: ūber — most laden, richest (superl., f. nom.)
glossary.gelido: gelidus — icy, cool (m. dat.); gelido fonti: to a cool spring
glossary.contermina: conterminus — bordering on, next to (f. nom.) + dat.
glossary.pacta: pactum — the things agreed, the plan (n. nom. pl.)
glossary.visa: videor — seeming (deponent perf. part., f. nom.); discedere visa: seeming to depart
glossary.isdem: īdem — from the same (abl. pl. = iisdem)
glossary.tenebras: tenebrae — darkness (acc. pl.); per tenebras: through the dark
glossary.versato: versō — turned (perf. part., n. abl.); versato cardine: the hinge turned
glossary.adopertaque: adoperiō — and having covered (perf. part., f. nom. + -que)
glossary.vultum: vultus — face (acc.); Greek acc. of respect: 'as to her face'
glossary.dictaque: dīcō — and appointed, agreed (perf. part., f. abl. + -que)
glossary.recenti: recēns — fresh, recent (abl.); recenti caede: with fresh killing
glossary.spumantis: spūmō — foaming (pres. part., acc. pl. = spumantes)
glossary.oblita: oblinō — besmeared, smeared (perf. part., f. nom.) + Greek acc.
glossary.depositura: dēpōnō — about to lay down (fut. part., f. nom.); 'meaning to slake'
glossary.procul: procul — far off, in the distance
glossary.lunae: lūna — of the moon (gen.); ad lunae radios: by the moon's rays
glossary.timido: timidus — fearful, frightened (m. abl.); timido pede: on fearful foot
glossary.velamina: vēlāmen — veil, covering (acc. pl.; poetic pl.)
glossary.lapsa: lābor — slipped, fallen (deponent perf. part., n. acc. pl.)
glossary.conpescuit: compescō — checked, quenched (perf.; older spelling conp-)
glossary.silvas: silva — the woods (acc. pl.); in silvas: into the woods
glossary.inventos: inveniō — found, come upon (perf. part., m. acc. pl.)
glossary.ipsa: ipse — (the woman) herself (f. abl.); sine ipsa: without its owner
glossary.ore: ōs — with the mouth (abl.); ore cruentato: with bloodied mouth
glossary.cruentato: cruentō — bloodied, blood-stained (perf. part., n. abl.)
glossary.egressus: ēgredior — having come out (deponent perf. part., m. nom.)
glossary.alto: altus — deep (m. abl.); in alto pulvere: in the deep dust
glossary.totoque: tōtus — and (over) the whole (n. abl. + -que); toto ore: over his whole face
glossary.tinctam: tingō — stained, dyed (perf. part., f. acc.)
glossary.una: ūnus — one (f. nom.); una nox: one night
glossary.e: ē — out of, from (= ex); e quibus: of whom
glossary.dignissima: dignus — most worthy (superl., f. nom.) + abl.
glossary.vita: vīta — of life (abl.); longa vita: of a long life
glossary.nostra: noster — our (f. nom.); poetic plural for 'my'
glossary.miseranda: miseror — poor, to be pitied (gerundive, f. voc.)
glossary.loca: locus — places (n. acc. pl.); in loca: into places
glossary.metus: metus — of fear (gen.); plena metus: full of dread
glossary.fero: ferus — savage, wild (m. abl.); fero morsu: with savage bite
glossary.viscera: viscera — the inward flesh, entrails (acc. pl.)
glossary.hac: hic — this (f. abl.); sub hac rupe: beneath this crag
glossary.timidi: timidus — of a coward (m. gen.); timidi est: it is a coward's part
glossary.pactae: pactus — of the agreed, appointed (f. gen.); pactae arboris: of the appointed tree
glossary.notae: nōtus — to the well-known, familiar (f. dat.)
glossary.nostri: noster — of my/our (m. gen.); nostri sanguinis: of my blood
glossary.accinctus: accingō — girded, equipped (perf. part., m. nom.); quo accinctus erat: with which he was girt
glossary.ilia: īlia — the groin, lower belly (acc. pl.)
glossary.mora: mora — delay (nom.); nec mora: without delay
glossary.resupinus: resupīnus — lying on the back, sprawled (m. nom.)
glossary.aliter: aliter — otherwise; non aliter quam: just as
glossary.vitiato: vitiō — flawed, faulty (perf. part., n. abl.); vitiato plumbo: the lead being flawed
glossary.stridente: strīdeō — hissing, whistling (pres. part., n. abl.)
glossary.longas: longus — long (f. acc. pl.); longas aquas: long jets of water
glossary.ictibus: ictus — with strokes, in bursts (abl. pl.)
glossary.arborei: arboreus — of the tree (m. nom. pl.); arborei fetus: the tree's fruit
glossary.adspergine: aspergō — by the sprinkling, spray (abl.; older spelling adsp-)
glossary.atram: āter — black, dark (f. acc.); in atram faciem: to a dark hue
glossary.madefactaque: madefaciō — and soaked, drenched (perf. part., f. nom. + -que)
glossary.pendentia: pendeō — hanging (pres. part., n. acc. pl.); pendentia mora: the hanging berries
glossary.metu: metus — from fear (abl.); metu nondum posito: fear not yet laid aside
glossary.posito: pōnō — laid aside, set down (perf. part., m. abl.)
glossary.ne: nē — lest, so that not (+ subj.)
glossary.fallat: fallō — she should fail, disappoint (pres. subj.)
glossary.animoque: animus — and with the mind, heart (abl. + -que)
glossary.quantaque: quantus — and how great (n. acc. pl. + -que)
glossary.vitarit: vītō — she had escaped, avoided (perf. subj. = vitaverit)
glossary.sic: sīc — so, thus; ut… sic: as… so
glossary.facit: faciō — makes; facit incertam: makes (her) uncertain
glossary.cruentum: cruentus — bloody (n. acc.); cruentum solum: the bloody ground
glossary.pedem: pēs — foot (acc.); retro pedem tulit: drew her foot back
glossary.oraque: ōs — and the face (acc. pl. + -que; poetic pl.)
glossary.buxo: buxus — than boxwood (abl. of comparison); a pale wood
glossary.aequoris: aequor — of the sea (gen.); aequoris instar: like the sea
glossary.instar: īnstar — like, the equal of (indeclinable + gen.)
glossary.exigua: exiguus — slight, tiny (f. abl.); exigua aura: by a faint breeze
glossary.remorata: remoror — having lingered, delayed (deponent perf. part., f. nom.)
glossary.amores: amor — her love, her beloved (acc. pl.; poetic pl.)
glossary.claro: clārus — loud, clear (m. abl.); claro plangore: with loud beating
glossary.laniata: laniō — having torn (perf. part., f. nom.) + Greek acc.
glossary.comas: coma — the hair (acc. pl.); Greek acc. of respect
glossary.amplexaque: amplector — and having embraced (deponent perf. part., f. nom. + -que)
glossary.amatum: amō — beloved (perf. part., n. acc.); corpus amatum: the beloved body
glossary.cruori: cruor — with the blood (dat.); fletum cruori miscuit: mixed weeping with blood
glossary.gelidis: gelidus — cold (m. abl. pl.); gelidis in vultibus: on the cold face
glossary.vultibus: vultus — on the face (abl. pl.; poetic pl.)
glossary.figens: fīgō — planting, fixing (pres. part.); oscula figens: planting kisses
glossary.mihi: ego — to/from me (dat.); te mihi ademit: took you from me
glossary.iacentes: iaceō — lying, fallen (pres. part., acc. pl.); vultus iacentes: the fallen face
glossary.nomen: nōmen — name (acc.); ad nomen: at the name
glossary.a: ā — from, by (= ab); a morte: by death
glossary.gravatos: gravō — weighed down (perf. part., m. acc. pl.); a morte gravatos: heavy with death
glossary.visaque: videō — and (she) having been seen (perf. part., f. abl. + -que); visa illa: abl. abs.
glossary.quae: quī — and she (connecting rel., f. nom.); who, which
glossary.ense: ēnsis — of/from the sword (abl.); ense vacuum: empty of its sword
glossary.vacuum: vacuus — empty (n. acc.) + abl. of separation
glossary.unum: ūnus — one (thing) (n. acc.); in unum hoc: for this one act
glossary.vires: vīs — strength (acc. pl., from vīrēs); in vulnera vires: strength for the wounds
glossary.persequar: persequor — I will follow, pursue (deponent fut.)
glossary.extinctum: exstinguō — the dead man, extinguished (perf. part., m. acc.)
glossary.dicar: dīcō — I shall be called (pass. fut.)
glossary.tui: tū — of you (gen.); leti tui: of your death
glossary.me: ego — me (abl.); a me: from me
glossary.revelli: revellō — to be torn away (pass. inf.)
glossary.sola: sōlus — alone, only (f. abl.); morte sola: by death alone
glossary.estote: sum — be! (fut. imper. pl.); estote rogati: be entreated
glossary.rogati: rogō — asked, entreated (perf. part., m. nom. pl.)
glossary.illiusque: ille — and his/hers (gen. + -que); illius parentes: his parents
glossary.certus: certus — sure, faithful, true (m. nom.); certus amor: true love
glossary.hora: hōra — hour (nom.); hora novissima: the last hour
glossary.conponi: compōnō — to be laid together, buried together (pass. inf.; older spelling conp-)
glossary.invideatis: invideō — you should begrudge (pres. subj.) + inf.
glossary.eodem: īdem — the same (m. abl.); tumulo eodem: in the same tomb
glossary.unius: ūnus — of one (gen.); corpus unius: the body of one
glossary.es: sum — you are; es tectura: you are going to cover
glossary.tectura: tegō — going to cover (fut. part., f. nom.)
glossary.pullosque: pullus — and dark, blackish (m. acc. pl. + -que); pullos fetus: dark fruit
glossary.luctibus: lūctus — for mournings, grief (dat. pl.); luctibus aptos: fit for grief
glossary.aptos: aptus — fit, suited (m. acc. pl.) + dat.
glossary.gemini: geminus — of a twin, double (m. gen.); gemini cruoris: of a double bloodshed
glossary.aptato: aptō — fitted, set in place (perf. part., m. abl.); aptato mucrone: the point set
glossary.pectus: pectus — the breast (acc.); pectus sub imum: beneath the breast
glossary.mucrone: mucrō — with the point (of a sword) (abl.)
glossary.imum: īmus — lowest, bottom (n. acc.); sub imum pectus: beneath the lower breast
glossary.quodque: quī — and what, and that which (n. nom. + -que)
glossary.superest: supersum — survives, is left over (+ dat.)
glossary.urna: urna — in an urn (abl.); una in urna: in one urn
